summary: Jeff Atwood explores the famous 1978 Microsoft group photo, tracking down what happened to each of the 11 people pictured alongside Bill Gates and Paul Allen. The post reveals the diverse paths these early Microsoft employees took after leaving the company, from cattle ranching to developing Cabbage Patch dolls to psychedelic drug advocacy. Despite leaving a company that would become one of the most valuable in history, most accumulated modest fortunes in the millions while Gates and Allen became billionaires. The research draws from a 2000 Time article and other sources to piece together the fates of Microsoft's earliest team members.
key_points: The 11 people in Microsoft's 1978 photo took remarkably diverse career paths after leaving, including cattle ranching, environmental philanthropy, and golf course software
Most early Microsoft employees left within the first 5-7 years, with Marc McDonald notably leaving because the company was 'too big' in 1984
The wealth disparity between founders and early employees is stark: Gates at $50 billion versus most others at $1-20 million
One early employee (Marla Wood) sued Microsoft for sex discrimination after leaving
Microsoft's first employee, Marc McDonald, holds badge number 00001 and eventually rejoined through an acquisition
money_quotes: Marc McDonald: Programmer. Microsoft's first employee. Left Microsoft in 1984 because it was 'too big'
Bob O'Rear: Chief mathematician. Left Microsoft in 1993. Now a cattle rancher.
Bob Wallace: Production manager-designer. Left Microsoft in 1983. Was a psychedelic-drug advocate.
Marla Wood: Bookkeeper. Left Microsoft in 1980, then sued the company for sex discrimination. Now a self-described 'professional volunteer'.
key_insight: The early employees of what became one of history's most valuable companies largely cashed out early and pursued unconventional second acts, suggesting that being present at a tech revolution's birth doesn't guarantee—or even predict—staying for the ride.
